sourceRef.citation: Kourovka Notebook, Problem 19.79 (Khukhro & Mazurov eds., ed.19)

statement.latex: (T.\,Springer). Let$G$be a group. Suppose that for every integer$n > 0$the group$G$has a unique (up to isomorphism) irreducible complex linear representation of dimension$n$. (Note that$G = SL(2,{ Q})$has these properties, by a theorem of Borel--Tits (Ann. Math., { 97} (1973), 499--571).) Is it true that$G$has a normal subgroup$N$such that$G/N$is isomorphic to$SL(2,{ Q})$?

reiAssessment.solveRationale: Group theory VI_BRIDGING; Rei not specialized in group theory (yet)
reiAssessment.formalizationNotes: Kourovka problems are typically research-level group theory
